result.markdown:
--- title: "Flow Through a Contraction–Throat–Diffuser Duct" subtitle: "From Potential Flow to Viscous Navier–Stokes" author: "Irving Martinez" date: today format: html: code-fold: true code-tools: true self-contained: true toc: true toc-depth: 3 theme: darkly highlight-style: dracula jupyter: python3 --- ## Introduction This document provides a comprehensive derivation and simulation of incompressible duct flow. We explore the transition from inviscid potential theory to the viscous Navier-Stokes equations, focusing on the mechanics of acceleration and pressure recovery. --- ## §1 The Geometry The duct follows a contraction–throat–diffuser profile defined by a smooth cosine contraction and a linear expansion. ::: {#6e22b2cd .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import plotly.graph_objects as go from scipy.integrate import solve_ivp def duct_h(x, h_in=0.36, h_t=0.14, angle_deg=8.0): x_c, x_t = 0.35, 0.45 alpha = np.tan(np.radians(angle_deg)) x = np.atleast_1d(x) h = np.empty_like(x) h[x < x_c] = h_in + (h_t - h_in) * 0.5 * (1 - np.cos(np.pi * x[x < x_c] / x_c)) h[(x >= x_c) & (x < x_t)] = h_t h[x >= x_t] = h_t + alpha * (x[x >= x_t] - x_t) return h x_arr = np.linspace(0, 1, 500) h_arr = duct_h(x_arr) plt.figure(figsize=(10, 3), facecolor='#0f1117') ax = plt.gca() ax.set_facecolor('#141820') ax.fill_between(x_arr, -h_arr, h_arr, color='#4a9eff', alpha=0.2) ax.plot(x_arr, h_arr, color='#8ab0d0', lw=2) ax.plot(x_arr, -h_arr, color='#8ab0d0', lw=2) plt.title("Duct Geometry", color='white') plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](navier_stokes_duct_files/figure-html/cell-2-output-1.png){width=805 height=283} ::: ::: --- ## §2 Potential Flow and Bernoulli In inviscid flow, the speed $\bar{U}(x)$ is inversely proportional to $h(x)$. Bernoulli's principle states $p + \frac{1}{2}\rho U^2 = \text{const}$. ::: {#bd1881b7 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} Q = 0.08 rho = 1.0 U_bar = Q / (2 * h_arr) p_arr = 1.0 + 0.5 * rho * (U_bar[0]**2 - U_bar**2) fig = go.Figure() fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=x_arr, y=U_bar, name='Velocity Ū')) fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=x_arr, y=p_arr, name='Pressure p')) fig.update_layout(template='plotly_dark', title="Quasi-1D Potential Flow") fig.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: --- ## §3 The Navier–Stokes Equations We consider steady, incompressible flow: $$ \rho(\mathbf{u}\cdot\nabla)\mathbf{u} = -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^2\mathbf{u} $$ In the contraction, convection dominates; in the diffuser, the adverse pressure gradient and viscosity can lead to separation. --- ## §4 Viscosity and the Boundary Layer Viscosity causes the no-slip condition at the wall, creating parabolic velocity profiles. ::: {#bdf96a5e .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} def profile(y, h, U_avg): return 1.5 * U_avg * (1 - (y/h)**2) h_throat = duct_h(0.4)[0] U_throat = Q / (2 * h_throat) y_vals = np.linspace(-h_throat, h_throat, 100) u_vals = profile(y_vals, h_throat, U_throat) plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4)) plt.plot(u_vals, y_vals) plt.title("Viscous Velocity Profile at Throat") plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](navier_stokes_duct_files/figure-html/cell-4-output-1.png){width=516 height=357} ::: ::: --- ## §5 Particle Trajectories (Animated) We integrate the Lagrangian paths $\frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = \mathbf{u}$. ::: {#3d21536d .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} import numpy as np import plotly.graph_objects as go from scipy.integrate import solve_ivp def get_vel(x, y, Q=0.08, mode='viscous'): h = duct_h(x)[0] u_avg = Q / (2*h) u = 1.5 * u_avg * (1 - (y/h)**2) if mode=='viscous' else u_avg # Approximation for v via continuity dx = 1e-4 dh_dx = (duct_h(x+dx)[0] - h) / dx v = -y * (u_avg/h) * dh_dx return u, v n_particles = 9 y0s = np.linspace(-0.25, 0.25, n_particles) t_eval = np.linspace(0, 2.5, 120) frames = [] particle_data = [solve_ivp(lambda t, p: get_vel(p[0], p[1]), [0, 2.5], [0, y], t_eval=t_eval).y for y in y0s] for k in range(len(t_eval)): frame_pts = [] for p in particle_data: frame_pts.append(go.Scatter(x=[p[0, k]], y=[p[1, k]], mode='markers', marker=dict(size=6))) frames.append(go.Frame(data=frame_pts, name=str(k))) fig_anim = go.Figure(data=frames[0].data, frames=frames) fig_anim.update_layout(xaxis=dict(range=[0, 1]), yaxis=dict(range=[-0.5, 0.5]), updatemenus=[dict(type="buttons", buttons=[dict(label="Play", method="animate", args=[None])])], template='plotly_dark') fig_anim.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: --- ## §6 Final Summary Dashboard A unified view of the duct's performance fields. ::: {#39a21ac8 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} fig, axs = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(10, 8), sharex=True) axs[0].plot(x_arr, U_bar, color='lime', label='Velocity') axs[0].set_ylabel("m/s") axs[1].plot(x_arr, p_arr, color='orange', label='Pressure') axs[1].set_ylabel("Pa") plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](navier_stokes_duct_files/figure-html/cell-6-output-1.png){width=829 height=633} ::: :::
